entries.text:
**Relative potency** (vs hydrocortisone): glucocorticoid **5×**, mineralocorticoid **0** (table 50-2 G&G14). **Intermediate duration of action** (biological t½ 12-36 h).

**Triamcinolone acetonide** is one of the most widely used forms:
• **Intra-articular/intra-lesional**: local depot for osteoarthritis/bursitis, duration 4-6 weeks.
• **ICS** (Azmacort): equipotent to beclomethasone, an alternative in persistent asthma.
• **Oral topical** (Kenalog in Orabase) for aphthous stomatitis.
• **Intravitreal** in macular edema.

**Typical local ADRs**: cutaneous/subcutaneous atrophy with superficial infiltration, depigmentation of the site, rarely septic arthritis from contamination. Systemic effects only at high doses or with absorption from large surface areas.

**Definition** (G&G14): "the consequence of excessive generalized stimulation of 5-HT receptors, especially when SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, MAOIs, and triptans are combined".

**Clinical triad** (**Hunter** criteria, sensitivity 84%, specificity 97%):
1. **Altered mental status**: agitation, hypomania, confusion.
2. **Autonomic hyperactivity**: hyperthermia (even >40 °C), tachycardia, profuse sweating, mydriasis, shivering, diarrhea.
3. **Neuromuscular hyperactivity**: tremor, **spontaneous or inducible clonus** (especially in the lower limbs), hyperreflexia, rigidity.

**Most frequently implicated drugs**:
• **MAOI + SSRI/SNRI/TCA** (most dangerous combination — mandatory washout of 2-5 weeks after fluoxetine)
• Triptans + SSRI/SNRI (overestimated but real risk)
• Tramadol, meperidine, fentanyl, ondansetron, linezolid, methylene blue, MDMA

**Differential diagnosis**: vs **neuroleptic malignant syndrome** (NMS) — slower rigidity, no clonus, onset over days; vs **malignant hyperthermia** — exposure to halogenated anesthetics/succinylcholine.

**Treatment**: discontinuation of all serotonergic agents, supportive care (cooling, hydration, BZD for agitation), and in severe cases **cyproheptadine** 12 mg + 2 mg every 2 h (5-HT2A/H1 antagonist) or IV **chlorpromazine**. Intubation + paralysis in severe cases.

**Detailed mechanism** (G&G14): blockade of **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** in the inactivated state (use-dependent) → ↓ glutamate release. Also modulation of some HVA-type Ca²⁺ channels.

**Pharmacokinetics**:
• **Oral bioavailability**: ~98%.
• **Metabolism**: hepatic glucuronidation (UGT1A4) — **not CYP** → few classic-type interactions, but metabolism modulated by UGT inducers.
• **t½ ~25-33 h** in monotherapy; **becomes ~15 h** with inducers (CBZ, phenytoin, phenobarbital, rifampin); **becomes ~60 h** with valproate (UGT inhibitor) → high **rash risk** → **halved titration**.
• **Children < 2 years**: greater clearance → proportionally higher doses.

**FDA/EMA indications**:
• Epilepsy: focal seizures (mono- or add-on), generalized tonic-clonic, **Lennox-Gastaut syndrome**.
• Bipolar I disorder: **prevention of depressive relapses** (less effective on acute manic episodes).

**ADRs**:
• **Benign skin rash 10%** (usually within the first 8 weeks) → precautionary discontinuation.
• **Severe rash (SJS/TEN, DRESS, HLH) 0.1-1%** → slow titration is preventive. Children > adults.
• Dizziness, diplopia, ataxia, headache (dose-dependent).
• Generally "clean": no marked sedation, no weight ↑, no significant cognitive effects, mood-neutral. Reproductive profile **better** than most AEDs (but not nil).

**Standard titration scheme** (adults, without valproate): 25 mg/day × 2 weeks → 50 mg/day × 2 weeks → +50 mg every 1-2 weeks up to 100-200 mg/day. **With valproate**: 25 mg every other day × 2 weeks → 25 mg/day × 2 weeks → very slow titration. Never bypass → SJS.

**G&G14 profile**:
• **Spectrum**: similar to erythromycin but **more active against Haemophilus, Moraxella, Legionella, Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, atypical mycobacteria (MAC)**.
• **PK**: oral bioavailability **~55%** (meals slow but do not reduce it), t½ **3-7 h** → BID. Good tissue penetration (lung, tonsils, soft tissues).
• **Metabolism**: **CYP3A4** → **14-OH-clarithromycin** (active metabolite) + other metabolites.

**Clinically relevant interactions** (clarithromycin = **strong inhibitor of CYP3A4 and P-gp**):
• ↑ **digoxin** (renal P-gp inhibition, table G&G14 ch. 6) → toxicity.
• ↑ statins (atorva-, simva-, lova-) → rhabdomyolysis.
• ↑ warfarin (bleeding).
• ↑ cyclosporine, tacrolimus, sirolimus.
• ↑ theophylline, carbamazepine, ergotamines (ergotism).
• ⚠️ With **quinidine, disopyramide, antipsychotics** → ↑ risk of **torsades de pointes** (additive effect on QT).
• To be avoided with other QT↑ agents (FQ, class IA/III antiarrhythmics).

**Key indications**:
• Community-acquired pneumonia (atypical or susceptible S. pneumoniae).
• Pharyngotonsillitis/otitis media (alternative in β-lactam-allergic patients).
• **Triple anti-H. pylori regimen**: clarithromycin 500 mg + amoxicillin 1 g + PPI BID, 14 days (in areas with clarithromycin resistance <15%; otherwise quadruple therapy with bismuth).
• Prophylaxis and treatment of **MAC** in HIV/AIDS patients.
• Eradication of early **B. burgdorferi** in Lyme borreliosis.

**ADRs**: GI (less marked than erythromycin), metallic aftertaste, rare ↑ transaminases, allergies. **Pregnancy**: category C → avoid if possible (alternative: azithromycin). Dose adjustment in **severe CKD**.

**Human IgG2 anti-RANKL mAb** (G&G14): RANKL is the signal that activates osteoclasts → blocking it shuts down bone resorption.
• **Dose**: 60 mg SC every 6 months (osteoporosis); 120 mg every 4 weeks in bone metastases.
• Unlike bisphosphonates it does NOT deposit in bone → **reversible** effect: with abrupt discontinuation → osteoclastic **rebound** with **multiple vertebral fractures** → always sequence with a bisphosphonate.
• Usable in **renal failure** (no renal elimination).
• ADRs: **hypocalcemia** (correct Ca²⁺/vit D first), **ONJ** (osteonecrosis of the jaw), atypical femoral fractures, skin infections.

**Enolic (oxicam)** NSAIDs with a **long half-life** → once-daily dosing (piroxicam t½ ~50 h).
• **Meloxicam** = **COX-2 preferential** at low doses (less gastric injury); **piroxicam** = non-selective and among the most **gastrotoxic** NSAIDs.
• Hepatic metabolism via **CYP2C9** (interactions with warfarin; slow metabolizers).
• ADRs: typical of NSAIDs (GI, renal, cardiovascular); the prolonged persistence accentuates piroxicam's gastric risk.

**Potent synthetic mineralocorticoid** (9α-fluoro-hydrocortisone): mineralocorticoid activity ~125-150× hydrocortisone, modest glucocorticoid activity.
• **Indications** (G&G14): replacement therapy for **Addison disease/adrenal insufficiency** (with a glucocorticoid), **congenital adrenal hyperplasia** with salt wasting, **orthostatic hypotension/dysautonomia**.
• **Dose**: 0.05-0.2 mg/day orally.
• Mechanism: ↑ Na⁺ reabsorption + ↑ K⁺/H⁺ excretion in the distal tubule.
• ADRs: hypertension, edema, **hypokalemia**, alkalosis → monitor blood pressure and electrolytes.

**Glycopeptide** related to vancomycin (G&G14): same mechanism (binds the **D-ala-D-ala** of peptidoglycan), Gram+ spectrum (MRSA, enterococci, C. difficile orally).
• Advantages: **long half-life** → 1 dose/day (IM or IV), **no 'red man syndrome'**, **less nephrotoxicity** than vancomycin.
• Available in **Europe** (not in the USA).
• **VanA cross-resistance** with vancomycin; monitoring of levels is useful in prolonged treatments.

**Sulfonanilide COX-2 preferential** NSAID (selectivity similar to celecoxib; G&G14) → good gastric tolerability.
• ⚠️ Documented **idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity** → AIFA: **maximum duration 15 days**, 100 mg BID, contraindicated in liver disease; **not available in the USA** precisely because of the hepatic risk.
• Indications: acute pain, dysmenorrhea, short-term osteoarticular inflammation.

**Chimeric anti-TNF-α mAb** (human Fc + murine Fab): neutralizes **soluble and membrane-bound** TNF-α → RA, Crohn disease/ulcerative colitis, psoriasis, spondyloarthritis.
• **IV** every 6-8 weeks; the murine component makes it **immunogenic** → **anti-drug antibodies (ADAs)** and infusion reactions → combined with **methotrexate** to reduce immunogenicity.
• ⚠️ Before starting: **screening for latent TB** and hepatitis B (risk of reactivation); risk of serious infections and (rarely) lymphoma.

**First oral direct renin inhibitor** for hypertension (G&G14): blocks upstream the conversion of angiotensinogen → Ang I.
• **Very low bioavailability (~2.6%)**.
• ADRs: **hyperkalemia**, diarrhea (dose-dependent), rare angioedema.
• ⚠️ **Contraindicated with ACE inhibitors/ARBs in diabetics** (**ALTITUDE** trial: ↑ renal/cardiovascular events) and in **pregnancy** (like all RAAS blockers). Use is marginal today.

Inhibits bacterial **dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR)** → blocks regeneration of **tetrahydrofolate**; together with the sulfonamide it achieves a **sequential blockade** of the folate pathway (co-trimoxazole).
• **Selectivity**: affinity for bacterial DHFR ≫ human.
• Alone: uncomplicated cystitis. In combination: UTIs, **Pneumocystis jirovecii**, nocardiosis.
• ADRs: **hyperkalemia** (blocks the **ENaC** channels of the distal tubule, amiloride-like effect — especially at high doses, CKD, elderly), megaloblastic anemia (reversible with **folinic acid**), rash.

**First-generation cephalosporins** (cefazolin IV, cephalexin oral; G&G14): spectrum mostly **Gram+** (MSSA staphylococci, streptococci) + some Gram- (E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus).
• **Key indication: surgical prophylaxis** — cefazolin IV within **60 min** of incision (covers cutaneous Gram+ organisms of the surgical site).
• They do **not** cross the blood-brain barrier → **not for meningitis**.
• Good tolerability; cross-allergy with penicillins is low (~1-2%; avoid in case of penicillin anaphylaxis).

**Zolpidem, zaleplon, zopiclone/eszopiclone** — 'Z' hypnotics, non-BZD but GABA-A agonists **selective for the α1 subunit** (G&G14) → **hypnotic** effect without appreciable anxiolytic/muscle-relaxant/anticonvulsant action.
• Rapid onset, short half-life (zaleplon very short → useful for nocturnal awakenings) → little 'hangover'.
• Signature ADR: **complex parasomnias** (sleepwalking, sleep-driving with amnesia).
• Tolerance/dependence lower than BZDs but not absent; antagonized by **flumazenil**.

**Trazodone, nefazodone** (SARI): **5-HT2A** antagonism + weak SERT inhibition + **H1/α1** blockade (G&G14).
• **Low-dose trazodone = hypnotic** (H1/α1 sedation dominates), at high doses an antidepressant; no dependence → widely used for **insomnia**.
• ADRs: sedation, orthostatic hypotension, **priapism** (rare, urological emergency).
• **Nefazodone**: withdrawn in many countries for severe **hepatotoxicity**.

**Venlafaxine, desvenlafaxine** — **SNRIs** (G&G14): reuptake of serotonin and, at higher doses, norepinephrine.
• **Dose-dependent effect**: low doses ≈ SSRI; with increasing doses the **noradrenergic** component is added (useful in neuropathic pain, like duloxetine).
• ADRs: nausea, insomnia, sexual dysfunction, **dose-dependent ↑ blood pressure** (monitor); marked **discontinuation syndrome** (short half-life) → taper slowly.

**Carbenicillin, ticarcillin** — extended-spectrum anti-**Pseudomonas** penicillins (carboxyl group; G&G14).
• Active against many Gram- organisms including Pseudomonas, but susceptible to β-lactamases → often combined with an inhibitor (**ticarcillin + clavulanate**).
• Today largely **supplanted by the ureidopenicillins** (piperacillin/tazobactam), which are more potent.
• ADRs: high **Na⁺ load** (heart failure), platelet dysfunction/bleeding, hypokalemia, hypersensitivity.

**Etifoxine** — non-BZD anxiolytic: acts on the mitochondrial **TSPO (18 kDa)** receptor → ↑ **neurosteroids** that positively modulate GABA-A (a site different from BZDs), plus a direct action on GABA-A.
• Advantages over BZDs: less sedation, **less dependence/withdrawal**, does not impair memory.
• ⚠️ Reports of **hepatotoxicity** and severe cutaneous reactions (DRESS / Stevens-Johnson) → contraindicated in liver disease (EMA/AIFA warnings).
_(Not present in Goodman & Gilman: a drug used predominantly in Europe.)_
